meta.name: Sourdough Country Loaf
meta.subtitle: Classic crusty boule/batard • fresh milled or commercial flour

source.notes: Canonical baseline for a classic sourdough country loaf with a strong crust and open, chewy crumb.

yield.notes: 1 medium loaf (~900g baked). Shape as a boule or batard.

optional: pizza stone + steam pan (alt bake method)

tasteProfile.notes: Balanced tang (depending on fermentation), wheaty aroma, crackly crust.

ingredients.label: Bread flour (or strong all-purpose)
ingredients.notes: For fresh milled flour, see Fresh-Milled Notes (you may need higher hydration and/or a blend).

ingredients.label: Whole wheat flour (fresh milled or commercial)
ingredients.notes: Adds strength, flavor, and fermentation activity. You can increase to 100g for a more rustic loaf.

ingredients.label: Water (room temp to slightly warm, ~75–82°F / 24–28°C)
ingredients.notes: This is 75% hydration based on total flour (500g). Hold back 25g and add during mixing if dough can take it.

ingredients.label: Active sourdough starter (100% hydration, bubbly/at peak)
ingredients.notes: 100g starter contributes ~50g flour + ~50g water to the dough.

steps.title: Build/refresh starter (if needed)
instructions: Use starter at or near peak activity: doubled, bubbly, and elastic.
If your starter is sluggish, feed it 4–8 hours before mixing (timing depends on room temperature).

steps.title: Autolyse (flour + water rest)
instructions: In a large bowl, mix bread flour + whole wheat flour with 350g of the water until no dry flour remains.
Cover and rest 30–60 minutes.
This improves gluten development and helps achieve a more open crumb.

steps.title: Mix in starter + salt
instructions: Add the active starter to the autolysed dough and pinch/fold until mostly incorporated.
Dissolve salt in the remaining 25g water (or part of it) and add to the dough.
Mix by hand (Rubaud method or folds) until the dough feels cohesive and slightly elastic.

steps.title: Bulk fermentation with stretch-and-folds
instructions: Bulk ferment 3.5–5 hours at warm room temp (~75–80°F / 24–27°C).
During the first 2 hours, perform 3–4 sets of stretch-and-folds (or coil folds) every 30 minutes.
After folds, let the dough rise undisturbed until it looks puffy with visible bubbles and has risen ~30–60%.

steps.title: Pre-shape + bench rest
instructions: Turn dough onto a lightly floured surface.
Pre-shape into a loose round (boule) or log (batard).
Rest 15–25 minutes uncovered to relax gluten.

steps.title: Final shape + place in banneton
instructions: Shape into a tight boule or batard, creating surface tension.
Place seam-side up in a floured banneton (rice flour helps prevent sticking) or a towel-lined bowl.
Cover.

steps.title: Cold proof (recommended) or room-temp proof
instructions: Cold proof: refrigerate 8–16 hours for improved flavor and easier scoring.
Room-temp option: proof 60–120 minutes until slightly puffy and dough slowly springs back when gently pressed.

steps.title: Preheat oven + Dutch oven
instructions: Place Dutch oven (with lid) in oven and preheat to 475°F (245°C) for 30–45 minutes.
A thoroughly preheated vessel is key to oven spring.

steps.title: Score + bake (Dutch oven method)
instructions: Turn the cold dough out onto parchment (seam-side down).
Score with a lame/razor: one bold slash for a batard, or a cross/curve for a boule.
Transfer dough (with parchment) into the hot Dutch oven, cover with lid.
Bake 20 minutes covered at 475°F (245°C).
Remove lid, reduce to 450°F (232°C), and bake 18–25 minutes uncovered until deep brown.
Optional doneness check: internal ~205–210°F (96–99°C).

steps.title: Cool completely
instructions: Cool on a rack at least 2 hours before slicing.
Cutting too early can make the crumb gummy.

freshMilledNotes.summary: Fresh milled flour can ferment faster and absorb more water; bran can weaken gluten and reduce openness unless managed.
guidelines: If using 100% fresh milled flour, consider a blend (e.g., 70–85% fresh milled + 15–30% strong bread flour) for better loft.
Increase hydration gradually (add 10–30g extra water) if dough feels stiff after mixing; stop once dough becomes slack and hard to shape.
Extend autolyse to 60 minutes for fresh milled flour.
If crumb is dense, shorten bulk slightly (avoid over-fermentation) and improve shaping tension.

storage.counter: Store cut-side down on a board or loosely wrapped 1–2 days for best crust. After that, bag it to prevent drying.
storage.freezer: Slice and freeze up to 2–3 months. Toast from frozen or thaw at room temperature.
storage.refresh: To re-crisp: spritz crust lightly with water and bake at 400°F (205°C) for 8–12 minutes.

variations.name: More rustic (higher whole wheat)
variations.change: Use 400g bread flour + 100g whole wheat flour. Increase water to 385–400g as needed.
variations.notes: More wheaty flavor; slightly tighter crumb.

variations.name: Higher hydration (more open crumb)
variations.notes: Requires stronger flour and good folding/shaping; can be stickier with fresh milled flour.

variations.name: Same-day bake (no cold proof)
variations.change: After shaping, proof at room temp 60–120 minutes until dough slowly springs back, then bake.
variations.notes: Milder flavor; scoring is a bit trickier because dough is warmer/softer.

variations.name: Steam-pan method (no Dutch oven)
variations.change: Bake on a preheated stone/steel at 475°F with a steam pan for 20 minutes, then remove steam and bake at 450°F until dark.
variations.notes: Requires careful steam management; Dutch oven is most consistent.

notes: Bulk time is temperature- and starter-dependent. Watch the dough (rise, bubbles, feel), not the clock.
Over-proofing shows as a very fragile dough that doesn’t spring back and can collapse or spread.
Under-proofing shows as tight crumb and explosive tearing beyond the score.

audit.testNotes: Canonical classic sourdough country loaf with Dutch oven bake and optional cold proof for flavor and crust.
